r_relatable_problem.primary_problem: Insider leaks → Volume spikes → Massive profits (1,000%+)
relatability_triggers: It starts with a phone call... or maybe a little chat over some drinks. First one person, then another. Innocent chatter? Maybe not. Some of it is downright illegal.

s_specific_solution.core_mechanism: The Volume Spike Indicator (Rogue Trader System): In 1998 I developed a technical system called the Volume Spike Indicator: to catch insider leaks as they're happening. When there's heavy volume and no official news on a stock, people in the know are talking. VOLUME LEADS PRICE. This is Profit Secret #1 of my system. When a company is on the verge of big news, certain people find out. In cases like Enron or ImClone, the insiders found out bad news was coming. And they sold. In the case of LCA Vision, they knew good news was coming. So they bought. If you're watching this information, that's how you know when insiders are dumping or stocking up on shares. If there's enough of this activity, it causes a blip in the volume activity on the stock. A blip that can be seen by the trained eye. When you see changes in trading volume, it can be just as good as having inside information. With a little research, you can tell whether insiders are expecting good news or bad news, and you can act on that information.

IBM insider sales: Lou Gerstner sold $400M as Chairman
Global Crossing: Gary Winnick sold $734M before crash

headline.main_headline: When People Can't Keep Their Mouths Shut, You Can Make A Fortune
headline.subheadline: Blabbermouths helped us turn $10,000 into $113,200 on one trade alone. And because people can't help but talk, you can make profits like this over and over again...
headline.deck_copy: Thanks to 'loose lips,' you could have turned $10,000 into $113,200 with LCA Vision... $5,000 into $20,000 with AVI, made 2-1/2 times your money on BBH, doubled your money on Zi Corp., made 160% on Headwaters, and made 67% in just 8 days on Internap... just for starters...

schema_analysis.expectation_violated: Insider trading is illegal and inaccessible to regular investors. You can't profit from it. Yet this promises to legally profit from illegal insider activity.

headline.vsl_adaptation: Perfect for VSL - open with Martha Stewart/Sam Waksal ImClone story, reveal volume spike indicator, demonstrate case studies with charts

lead.full_text: Dear Investor, It starts with a phone call... or maybe a little chat over some drinks. First one person, then another. Innocent chatter? Maybe not. Some of it is downright illegal. But first one person acts on a hush-hush 'buy this stock' or 'sell that stock' tip. Then another. Pretty soon there's some serious action. Everyone wants to get in on some insider action. But the public doesn't know about it. 100 million investors watch stocks... and basically everyone has access to the same information. Or they're supposed to, in order to keep things fair. Thing is, sometimes people on the inside have more information than the average investor. They can keep it to themselves. Under certain conditions, they can even use the information for themselves -legally. But they're not supposed to give that information out before it becomes available to everyone. Again, to keep things fair. Problem is, people love to gossip. And they love to be the big shot. And they like to get friends or business associates in on a good thing. So they talk.
lead.hook_sentence: It starts with a phone call... or maybe a little chat over some drinks. First one person, then another. Innocent chatter? Maybe not. Some of it is downright illegal.
lead.transition_to_body: They talk... the market starts to act... and you get to profit...

three_part_structure.what_they_think_problem_is: I'm not connected to insider information networks. I don't have access to what's really happening inside companies before news becomes public. The market is fair and everyone has access to the same information at the same time.
three_part_structure.what_real_problem_is: People with inside information CAN'T keep their mouths shut. They gossip to friends, family, business associates, doctors, even cab drivers. This creates volume spikes BEFORE official news is released - visible signals of illegal insider activity happening in real-time. These volume spikes are public information (completely legal to track), but 99% of investors don't know how to read them or where to look.
three_part_structure.why_nothing_worked: Traditional investing advice assumes fair markets with simultaneous information access. But insiders leak information before official announcements through gossip networks. By the time news hits the wire, insiders have already acted. Without a system to detect volume spikes (the footprints of insider leaks), you're always trading AFTER the insiders, buying at tops and selling at bottoms. Meanwhile, insiders at companies like Enron, Tyco, ImClone, WorldCom, and Global Crossing sold billions while the public bought, because they knew catastrophic news was coming.

villain_profile.primary_villain: Insiders who illegally leak information (CEOs, board members, executives) + middlemen who protect them
villain_profile.villain_quote: Enron Chairman Kenneth Lay and 29 senior execs sold over $1 billion in stock while phony accounting numbers kept the stock going up... Tyco CEO Dennis Kozlowski sold about $86 million before the price dropped from over $60 to $12... Lou Gerstner dumped almost $400 million of IBM stock while Chairman of the Board... Michael Cowpland allegedly controlled a company that sold $20 million of Corel stock just before it posted poor earnings... Global Crossing CEO Gary Winnick sold $734 million before his company hit bottom.
villain_profile.secondary_villain: The system that allows insiders to act on information while the public remains in the dark

Volume spikes reveal when insiders leak information through gossip. One indicator detects all insider activity.
narrative_reorganization.new_self_concept: From 'regular investor trading in the dark' to 'investigator who sees insider footprints through volume analysis - legally profiting from their illegal gossip networks'

mechanism.name: The Volume Spike Indicator (Rogue Trader System)
mechanism.explanation_text: In 1998 I developed a technical system called the Volume Spike Indicator: to catch insider leaks as they're happening. When there's heavy volume and no official news on a stock, people in the know are talking. VOLUME LEADS PRICE. This is Profit Secret #1 of my system. When a company is on the verge of big news, certain people find out. In cases like Enron or ImClone, the insiders found out bad news was coming. And they sold. In the case of LCA Vision, they knew good news was coming. So they bought. If you're watching this information, that's how you know when insiders are dumping or stocking up on shares. If there's enough of this activity, it causes a blip in the volume activity on the stock. A blip that can be seen by the trained eye. When you see changes in trading volume, it can be just as good as having inside information. With a little research, you can tell whether insiders are expecting good news or bad news, and you can act on that information.

specimens.quote: On December 27, 2001, Martha Stewart got a tip that Sam Waksal (CEO of ImClone and her personal friend) was trying to dump his shares in a panic. Martha sold the same day, cashing out a position worth US$250,000. The next day, at 3:00 P.M., the FDA sent a fax to ImClone rejecting their promising cancer drug, Erbitux. ImClone's stock promptly crashed. Within 72 hours of Martha's sale, shares of ImClone lost an estimated US$1.5 billion in valuation... The day Martha sold her ImClone stock, more than 7.7 million shares traded - the highest number of shares in over two years.

I'd been watching a company called LCA Vision (they have a chain of laser vision correction clinics) for the better part of two years... Slowly, steadily, the volume began to increase. Then there were some volume spikes that really looked interesting. First in late October/early November, and again in late November/early December. Then a very strong spike in early January... I started recommending LCA Vision in late 1998... Within seven months, LCA Vision skyrocketed from $1.06 a share to over $14 a share... When the stock hit $12 a share I figured it was time to cash out... Subscribers who followed that early recommendation could have turned $10,000 into $113,200 in a little over seven months - a remarkable 1,032% profit.

On August 20, more than 1 million shares of a company called Neoware traded hands. That was almost as much as the previous five days combined! And it was the stock's fourth heaviest volume day for the year. Something was up... So I recommended the stock at about US$14.70 a share. Eight days later, Neoware announced record sales and record profits for its 4th quarter... When I sold, shares of Neoware got as high as US$18.85... for a quick and easy profit of 26%! Heck, it didn't stop there. A day after my sale, Neoware reached a high of US$19.50 - a gain of 33% in just 9 days!

On June 23, 1999, our tracking system alerted us that volume spiked from 9,000 one day to 171,600 the next on Zi Corporation. A quick check revealed that there was no news to speak of. But over the next nine trading sessions, more than 633,000 shares traded hands... I issued a buy signal at under $5.25 a share. In the days that followed, three more announcements were made. The company announced licensing agreements with two of China's largest electronics manufacturing companies... Zi Corp. shares soared to over $10 in less than a month! My subscribers quickly doubled their investment.

I was satisfied. So on June 29 I issued a buy recommendation at under $20 on Metricom. Turned out my bulletin went out just as the Paul Allen deal was being announced. Five days later we took profits of 110% when the stock closed above our target at just over $42.

Take one of the biggest winners that year - Hemispherx Biopharma. On June 29, some 730,000 shares traded hands. It was a huge spike in volume, and obviously, some big players were buying up shares... And if you had found what lay in those files, you would have bought early on the morning of June 30 - before the mass market caught on. And you'd have picked up Hemispherx Biopharma for just $4.50 a share. A scant 7 days later, investors started jumping on the band wagon. Hemispherx Biopharma's stock jumped to $8 - handing the early investors a juicy 77% profit in just one week.

Audio Highway was a company producing and selling audio books over the Internet... Suddenly on March 18, 1999, over 6 million shares traded in a single day! Again, nothing that was announced could justify such a dramatic surge in interest. When I put out a recommendation the stock was at $11. Two weeks later the company revealed that it had struck a deal with Microsoft to pilot an audio books channel on Microsoft's Windows Media.com website. The stock rocketed up to $34 - a 209% profit in just over two weeks!

All of these stock crashes - that cost investor hundreds of billions of dollars - were tipped by massive insider trading: Enron Chairman Kenneth Lay and 29 senior execs sold over $1 billion in stock while phony accounting numbers kept the stock going up. Tyco CEO Dennis Kozlowski sold about $86 million before price dropped from over $60 to $12. Lou Gerstner of IBM dumped almost $400 million while Chairman. Global Crossing CEO Gary Winnick sold $734 million before his company hit bottom.

But you never have to worry about owning a Tyco, ImClone, Global Crossing, Enron, or World Com (they lost 88.95%, 91%, 99.94%, 99.9%, 99.98% respectively), and even IBM (it dropped 47.82% in 30 months). Between them, investors lost over $500 billion. All of these were tipped by massive insider trading. My 'inside information' has kept me and my readers out of every single one of the major Wall Street scandals of the last couple of years.

When e Fax.com had a huge volume spike my research found that the company had signed up an unprecedented 100,000 users in the last 30 days - and a press release was in the works. We recommended e Fax.com at $7.75. Two days later after the press release went out - e Fax.com shot up to $21 and ended the week at $31 a share. Rogue Traders walked away with a 250% profit.
Netegrity shares surged from $9.50 to over $20 after some unusual volume movement in March of last year. We took home 58% profits in two days when the stock hit $15.

Some AVI Warrants we were watching were trading a few thousand shares a day until mid-March '99, when 109,000 shares moved. We issued a buy with the warrant selling for 75 cents. Two weeks later, we sold for $2- a 166% profit.

When VTEL's trading volume went haywire, spiking to 1.5 million after trading in the 200,000 range. We bought with the price at $2.75. Three weeks later, the price shot to $7 on very heavy volume (3 million shares) the day after the rock group, The Smashing Pumpkins, used the company's technology for a live Webcast. Meanwhile, Rogue Traders walked away with 155% profits.

proof.strongest_proof_element: ImClone/Martha Stewart case: Documented by SEC, Waksal pleaded guilty, volume spike 7.7M shares (highest in 2 years) on Dec 27 with no official news, next day FDA rejection announced, stock crashed 91%, proves volume reveals insider leaks before official announcements
proof.proof_sequence: Intrigue opening (phone calls/gossip) → ImClone/Martha Stewart scandal (documented insider trading) → LCA Vision case study (1,032% gain) → Volume Spike Indicator explanation → Real-time Neoware trade (26% in 8 days) → Zi Corporation (100% in 30 days) → Metricom Paul Allen deal (110% in 5 days) → FDA approval predictions → Catastrophic loss avoidance (Enron/Tyco/WorldCom) → Additional winning trades → Testimonials

fssit_candidates.statement: Problem is, people love to gossip. And they love to be the big shot. And they like to get friends or business associates in on a good thing. So they talk.
fssit_candidates.past_explaining_power: Explains the entire insider trading ecosystem not as sophisticated conspiracy but as human weakness (ego/gossip). You weren't excluded from elite networks - you just didn't know insiders CAN'T keep secrets. Their need to brag/help friends leaves footprints (volume spikes) you can legally track. Every major move is preceded by gossip that shows up in volume.

fssit_candidates.statement: When insiders open their yaps, that's the best time to make money. A lot of it. Having insider information is a guarantee you'll make money. It's like a license to print money. That's why it's illegal. But it happens. And it happens all the time.
fssit_candidates.past_explaining_power: Explains why you could never win consistently. While you analyzed fundamentals, insiders already knew outcomes and acted in advance. They had a 'license to print money' through advance knowledge. You weren't bad at analysis - you were competing against people who already knew the answers. Martha Stewart, Sam Waksal, Kenneth Lay, Lou Gerstner - billions in profits from advance knowledge. Now you can see their footprints.

fssit_candidates.statement: 100 million investors watch stocks... and basically everyone has access to the same information. Or they're supposed to, in order to keep things fair. Thing is, sometimes people on the inside have more information than the average investor.
fssit_candidates.past_explaining_power: Explains the fundamental lie of 'fair markets.' You were taught everyone has access to the same information simultaneously. That's the theory. In practice, insiders leak information to friends/family/associates before official announcements. You weren't playing in fair markets - you were playing in markets where insiders front-run every major move through gossip networks.

rsf_analysis.fssit_placement_pattern: Primary FSSIT in opening (people can't keep mouths shut), amplified by ImClone scandal (documented illegal gossip), reinforced throughout every case study (volume spikes before news), validated by villain examples (billions sold by insiders who leaked information)

temporal_analysis.temporal_urgency_device: Recent scandals creating emotional urgency for revenge/vindication, current market opportunities being tracked
